transcript: Now my talk is titled as you can see here Fully Encrypted Solana Execution with Arcium. And for those of you who perhaps have not heard of Arcium before, or maybe if you have heard of it before, just based on that title that probably already spins up, you know, first what is that? Second, why should I care about this or why is this important? And lastly, most importantly, how does this work? And hopefully after that, how can I use this within my own products to add privacy? So first for the first part, fully encrypted. What do we mean by this? So generally speaking, you know, if you go to Solana today and you go to a block explorer and pull up any account or transaction or what have you that you like, you can see any information that you want about that there basically. Who the sender is, who the recipient is, what data was input, and so on. There's some stuff you can do to, you know, hide that a little bit, for example, not uploading your IDL, writing your code in such a way that it's difficult to reverse engineer. But these obfuscation techniques obviously are just, you know, a band-aid that doesn't really solve that problem. The other technique that you can of course say as well is that you store your data off-chain, which is what, you know, many institutions for example are forced to do today, and only interface with Solana for certain operations. But of course this isn't great either because that means you rely on a trusted third party. So what we're enabling with Arcium is that we allow you to use provable cryptography to have everything hidden. Meaning that instead of having an adversary have to capture a third party or have to, you know, de-obfuscate a little bit, your adversary would have to gain a Nobel Prize for breaking cryptography basically to be able to withdraw private information that you want to keep hidden. And yeah, when we say hide everything, we mean everything, meaning inputs, intermediary values, outputs, you know, the whole full thing. And yeah, the last aspect that we mean with fully encrypted as well is zero information leakage. Concretely what I mean by this is that traditionally when people think about privacy on general purpose blockchains, you think of mixers, but you know, even though these are based on cryptography, they don't have zero information leakage in the sense that if you deposit a thousand two hundred dollars into it and ten seconds later withdraw one thousand two hundred dollars once more, it's quite obvious to an outside observer using statistical methods what you're doing. The second part, Solana execution. I don't think I have to really explain what that is. Obviously we're at this conference for a reason. But concretely for our use case what this means is that any operation is executable that you'd be able to execute on Solana in an encrypted manner, not just a subset. And on top of that having direct compatibility with Solana. Meaning you don't have to go to a different chain or, you know, set up some off-chain thing or whatnot. Just your Solana smart contract lives just like a normal program on Solana. Your accounts, all the state lives on Solana. You execute things on Solana. So you know, direct compatibility. And yeah, the thing that we built to make this happen is Arcium. We sometimes like to call it the encrypted supercomputer. But more concretely in I guess a bit more layman's terms, it's a network extension for private state and private execution which is powered by Secure Multi-Party Computation or MPC for short. And yeah, we'll learn about how that works a little bit more. Before that real quick though, you know, now that we've said the what Arcium is and what it's used for, what properties it has, quickly just why should you care about this aside from it just being kind of cool I guess. The first part is, you know, wealth and payments. So, you know, as the presenters Andrew just said, generally speaking you don't want the entire world to know how much money you have on-chain. On one hand because it feels a little bit awkward, but also on the other hand because especially over the last year and a half there's been quite a few attacks in the real world against people who unfortunately were targeted because their balance was visible on-chain. So, you know, if you make this private, that kind of solves the problem because you won't get targeted if someone doesn't know if you have one dollar or a million dollars. Second part, private data management. I think this is quite general the term, but what I mean by this is that Solana has grown a lot in the sense that it's much faster, much cheaper, much more efficient, so it can support a lot of use cases nowadays even from traditional finance and other sectors. However, a lot of those have to still keep many of that off-chain because they don't want their private information or their clients' private information or whatnot to be visible on-chain. And the last part, MEV. At the highest level, MEV is really just that since a validator can see your transaction and see what state changes it'll effect, they can take advantage of that by, you know, putting stuff in front or behind or yeah, just doing taking advantage of that while block building. So of course if the state changes in the transaction are encrypted, not much MEV you can really do there. I won't say it solves it 100% because it's a very complex issue, but at least for the most common type of MEV such as sandwiching, it does work quite well. So you know, now that we've talked about what we're building, why we're building it, most important part, how does this stuff actually work, especially after such bold claims. So here we have a little picture of the, you know, high level overview of how this works. As you can see there on the very left we have Solana, then we have some stuff in the middle and back to Solana. So let's start from the left. The idea is basically that you have some account living on Solana and you want this account to contain some data that you want to keep private. So you encrypt it, upload it to the account and store it there. So nice. That means if someone, you know, tries to fetch it from the block explorer, they only see a ciphertext and not the plaintext that you're actually hiding inside of it. But now the more interesting part is you actually want to compute with this data instead of just storing it on-chain. So let's say we want to have a simple computation like adding five. What we basically do then is we take this data, this encrypted data, and split it into so-called MPC secret shares. Uh with this little three shares thing there. And an MPC secret share basically has the property that if you put it back together it gives you a secret. However, any of the individual shares doesn't leak any information about that secret. Then what we do is we take each of those shares, pass it to one of the nodes inside of the Arcium network. They compute on it, you know, using Secure Multi-Party Computation, produce the result, adding five for example in the example. And then after that re-encrypt it to a single ciphertext that basically encrypts the result of that computation on the initial encrypted data. The important thing here is the individual nodes in the middle here never learn what the private data is. They only ever see shares. Solana and anyone observing Solana only ever sees ciphertexts as well. So that means we are computing on data without anyone actually seeing what that data is. Now I realize this is a decently bold claim to make as well because it raises a lot of questions about, okay, how do we make this secure? Do we need to trust these nodes in the middle since it's not on Solana and so on? And the way that we do this is by using our MPC backend developed in-house by our cryptographers called Cerberus. And this is a general purpose MPC backend, meaning that as opposed to what people generally associate with MPC being signatures, you can calculate anything that you want on this. Be it a signature or be it a DeFi operation, be it whatever you might like. The two most important properties of this is dishonest majority and maliciously secure. So what these two words mean is dishonest majority means that you have to trust that one node in the entire cluster is honest as opposed to 51% or 66% as is more generally the case in blockchain consensus or other types of MPC for example. And malicious security means that we assume that malicious nodes can do literally anything. Meaning they can go offline, send incorrect data, mess with the network in, yeah, any one way that they like. And this essentially gives us very strong security assumptions because dishonest majority plus malicious security means that as long as one node is honest, the other nodes can do pretty much whatever they want, but will still produce or we still won't produce invalid outputs and private data still won't be revealed. This is particularly useful if you're building on Arcium and, you know, you think, ah, I'm not sure if I quite trust this network or something like this. You can just say I'll deploy a node myself. That means you then have at least one node that is honest fulfilling the dishonest majority assumption and therefore being sure that your private data stays private and that no incorrect outputs get produced by the network basically. And yeah, I know most of us here are developers, so concretely you're probably thinking, okay, you know, this cryptography stuff is cool and all, but how can I actually use this without, you know, being a cryptographer or having a degree in this or whatnot? And for this we built Arcis, which is the first Rust to MPC bytecode compiler. In Arcis we allow you to write regular Rust code and compile this down to Cerberus bytecode, Cerberus being the MPC backend that we wrote. So this means that if you want to write an encrypted program, you know, on Arcium on Solana, you don't need to have some cryptography background or actually fully understand how all these things functions and whatnot. You can write regular Rust code, you know, at the click of a button compile it down to, uh, yeah, Cerberus bytecode, deploy this to Solana and you're basically done. And yeah, I would be remiss if I, you know, had a whole tech talk and didn't at least have a little bit of code to show for this. So that's the next slide here. This is just a quick example of, you know, how generally speaking some Arcis code might look. The example here is a little voting program. And yeah, as you can see here, this is, you know, regular Rust code. The only difference is basically the inputs and the outputs here. As you can see, those are with the little Enc generic struct here, which basically just means you're taking in an encrypted struct, yeah, you're taking in encrypted data. And yeah, internally you're doing some operations on it and then at the end you return encrypted value once more. So yeah, in that sense, if you know Rust, you can use Arcium. And yeah, that means the last question to ask is now that we've learned what it is, why it's necessary, how it works, how can I use it in my own app? So for that we have two QR codes here. The left one is the Twitter. There you can of course follow, you know, the latest news on it and whatnot. But the more interesting one for developers is obviously the one on the right, which is our docs on how to actually build with it, which contains some examples, more information and whatnot. And yeah, if you're interested, feel free to scan it and help us complete our mission of having the majority of TPS on Solana be encrypted by next Breakpoint. Thank you.

enrichment.generated_query: Arcium official announcement blog technical documentation Solana Breakpoint 2025

tags.impact_reason: Arcium introduces a fully encrypted execution environment for Solana, which is a solid update that addresses privacy concerns and could unlock institutional use cases.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a fractured crystalline structure as the Semantic Anchor because it directly visualizes the core concept of 'Dishonest Majority' Multi-Party Computation (MPC). The central, intact crystal represents the secure, encrypted computation. It is held together and processed by a network of shards, where the majority are dark and glitching (the 'dishonest' nodes), while a minority are pure and glowing (the 'honest' nodes). This contrast illustrates how security is maintained despite a compromised network. For the integrated text, I chose scrambled words like 'INPUT' and 'STATE' projected onto the crystal's surface. This diegetically demonstrates Arcium's promise to make all on-chain data unreadable and private, directly referencing the 'fully encrypted' aspect of the story.
visual_idea.description: In a dark, digital abyss, a large, complex geometric crystal representing an encrypted Solana execution hovers, glowing faintly. It is surrounded and held in place by a swarm of smaller, fractured shards. The vast majority of these shards are dark, semi-transparent, and flicker with red digital noise and glitch effects—the 'dishonest majority'. However, a small minority of shards are pristine, glowing with a brilliant cyan light. These 'honest' shards emit beams of light that form a protective, computational lattice around the central crystal. On the facets of the main crystal, holographic text displaying terms like 'INPUT' and 'STATE' is visible, but the words are heavily refracted and scrambled into illegibility by the crystal's internal structure.

final_prompt.prompt_text: In a vast, dark, digital abyss, a colossal, complex geometric crystal, representing an encrypted Solana execution, hovers with a faint, ethereal glow. This monumental central crystal, composed of intricate, razor-sharp facets, is encased and upheld by a dynamic swarm of countless smaller, fractured shards. The overwhelming majority of these shards are dark, semi-transparent, and continuously flicker with intense red digital noise and chaotic glitch effects, symbolizing the 'dishonest majority'. In stark contrast, a select minority of pristine shards radiate a brilliant, pure cyan light. These 'honest' shards emit potent beams that converge to form a protective, computational lattice, meticulously shielding the central crystal. Holographic projections of the terms 'INPUT' and 'STATE' shimmer on the central crystal's multifaceted surface, their forms heavily refracted, distorted, and scrambled into profound illegibility by the crystal's complex internal structure. The scene is bathed in dramatic volumetric lighting, with ethereal light rays cutting through the digital darkness, enhancing the epic scale and depth of this digital fortress. The composition is vertical (3:4 aspect ratio), emphasizing immense height and leaving negative space at the top, portraying the subject as an imposing, monumental structure. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic contrast.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a fractured crystalline structure as the Semantic Anchor because it directly visualizes the core concept of 'Dishonest Majority' Multi-Party Computation (MPC). The central, intact crystal represents the secure, encrypted computation. It is held together and processed by a network of shards, where the majority are dark and glitching (the 'dishonest' nodes), while a minority are pure and glowing (the 'honest' nodes). This contrast illustrates how security is maintained despite a compromised network. For the integrated text, I chose scrambled words like 'INPUT' and 'STATE' projected onto the crystal's surface. This diegetically demonstrates Arcium's promise to make all on-chain data unreadable and private, directly referencing the 'fully encrypted' aspect of the story.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on a facet of the central cryptographic crystal. The frame captures the stark textural contrast on its surface: the perfect, clean geometry of the crystal is reflected upon by the chaotic red light of a nearby 'dishonest' glitching shard. Cutting across the frame, a sharp, pure beam of cyan light from an unseen 'honest' node enters the crystal, refracting into a beautiful, complex caustic pattern internally, demonstrating the integrity of the computation within despite the corrupting external influence. No text is visible.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A tightly framed, symmetrical macro shot, filling the 1:1 square aspect ratio, focusing on a single, prominent facet of a central cryptographic crystal. The surface of the crystal exhibits a stark textural contrast, where its perfect, clean, crystalline geometry is partially reflected by the chaotic, intense red light emanating from a nearby 'dishonest' glitching shard. Simultaneously, a sharp, pure beam of volumetric cyan light, originating from an unseen 'honest' node, dramatically cuts across the frame to pierce the crystal. This cyan beam internally refracts, creating a beautiful, intricate, and complex caustic pattern within the crystal, visually demonstrating the unwavering integrity of the computation despite the corrupting external influence of the red glitching shard. The crystal's facets are razor-sharp, with deep refractions and specular highlights, emphasizing a digital fortress aesthetic. The glitching shard shows pixelation and visual distortion, providing a vibrant contrast. Volumetric lighting fills the scene, highlighting dust motes and atmospheric haze around the light beams. No text is visible. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic composition.

editorial.headline: Arcium Deploys 'Dishonest Majority' MPC to Encrypt Solana Execution
editorial.standfirst: The pivot from Elusiv brings generalized confidential computing to mainnet, allowing institutions to process private data on-chain without trusting the network majority.
key_takeaways: **Cerberus Backend**: New MPC protocol secures data with a "1-of-N" trust model, meaning data remains safe even if only a single node is honest.
**Arcis Compiler**: Enables developers to write standard Rust code that compiles directly to MPC bytecode, eliminating the need for specialized cryptographic knowledge.
**Adoption Target**: CTO Nico Schapeler set a goal for the majority of Solana's TPS to be encrypted by Breakpoint 2026.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
For institutional capital, Solana’s transparency has always been a double-edged sword. While high throughput is attractive, the inability to shield order flow and proprietary strategies from on-chain observers—and predatory MEV bots—has forced major players to rely on off-chain execution or trusted third parties. **Arcium** (formerly Elusiv) is closing this gap with a generalized confidential computing layer that runs directly on Solana, promising to keep data encrypted *during* execution.

The technical breakthrough lies in Arcium’s **Cerberus** backend, a Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) engine designed with a "dishonest majority" security model. Unlike traditional MPC or consensus mechanisms that require a 51% or 66% honest majority, Cerberus guarantees data privacy as long as a *single* node in the cluster remains honest. For institutions, this completely changes the risk profile: a firm can simply operate one node in the cluster themselves, ensuring their private state remains secure regardless of the behavior of other network participants.

To drive adoption, Arcium has abstracted the cryptographic complexity via **Arcis**, a compiler that translates standard Rust code into MPC bytecode. This allows Solana developers to build encrypted applications—such as private prediction markets, dark pools, or blinded auctions—without needing a background in zero-knowledge proofs or cryptography. By keeping the execution logic compatible with Solana’s native account model, Arcium avoids the fragmentation of liquidity often seen with Layer 2 privacy solutions.

The implications for market structure are profound. By enabling "zero information leakage" computation, Arcium effectively neutralizes sandwich attacks and front-running for encrypted transactions. CTO **Nico Schapeler** was explicit about the project's ambition, stating the goal is not merely to offer a niche privacy tool, but to transition the majority of Solana’s transaction throughput to encrypted execution by next year.
